skill.name: azure-compliance
skill.description: Audit Azure environments for compliance, security posture, resource configuration, and Key Vault expiration issues using Azure Quick Review, Azure Resource Graph, and Key Vault metadata tools.
skill.summary: Assess Azure compliance posture and Key Vault expiration risk with structured, evidence-based reporting.
skill.author: microsoft

security_audit.summary: Static analysis produced many high-volume alerts, but most are false positives from Markdown examples, Azure resource identifiers, placeholders, and documentation links. The skill has a legitimate compliance-audit purpose, but it can enumerate Azure resources and includes Key Vault secret inspection and state-changing remediation examples, so publication should include a warning.

medium_findings.title: Key Vault Secret Value Exposure Risk
medium_findings.description: The fallback guidance uses broad Key Vault secret show commands while the stated goal is expiration metadata. That command can expose secret values in tool output if a user runs it without a narrowed query.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command guidance is directly present and relates to Key Vault secrets. The intent is legitimate auditing, but the unscoped command can disclose more than expiration metadata.

medium_findings.title: State-Changing Azure Remediation Examples
medium_findings.description: The remediation reference includes Azure CLI and PowerShell examples that update storage accounts, Key Vaults, web apps, SQL servers, and other resources. These are documentation examples, but an assistant could propose or run them without enough confirmation.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples are explicitly state-changing Azure commands. They are normal remediation guidance, so the risk depends on whether a tool executes them without user approval.

low_findings.title: Documentation Command Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: Most external command detections are Markdown command blocks, MCP tool names, or Azure CLI examples. I did not find evidence that the skill hides executable scripts or automatically runs shell commands.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged content is visible Markdown reference material. There are no scripts, install hooks, or hidden command execution files in the reviewed evidence.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Are Terminology Matches
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography findings point to headings, tables, and Key Vault terminology rather than implementation of weak algorithms. I did not find evidence of MD5, SHA-1, DES, or similar cryptographic use in the cited context.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines contain descriptive compliance text or links to Key Vault SDK references. The semantic context does not show weak cryptographic algorithm use.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URL Alerts Are Benign References
low_findings.description: The URL detections are official documentation links or placeholder Azure Key Vault endpoint patterns. They do not send data to an external collection service.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are documentation targets or placeholder service endpoints. No evidence shows network exfiltration or calls to unknown infrastructure.

low_findings.title: Certificate And Key File Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The sensitive-file findings match SDK import lines and Key Vault class names, not embedded private keys, certificates, or filesystem paths to secret material.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines show package imports and client builders only. I did not find hardcoded private keys, certificate blobs, or local key files.

low_findings.title: Cloud Inventory Queries Are Intended Behavior
low_findings.description: The reconnaissance-style findings are Azure inventory and Resource Graph queries used for compliance assessment. This is expected for the skill, but results may contain resource names, roles, and configuration metadata.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The skill is explicitly designed to enumerate Azure compliance metadata. This is legitimate, but it still creates privacy and least-privilege considerations.

dangerous_patterns.title: Broad Secret Inspection Command
dangerous_patterns.description: The fallback Key Vault guidance includes secret show commands without restricting output to expiration attributes. Prefer metadata-only queries and avoid printing secret values.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command is present in the fallback table and targets secrets. The danger is exposure through output, not malicious code in the skill.

dangerous_patterns.title: Azure Resource Mutation Examples
dangerous_patterns.description: The remediation patterns include update, create, and enable commands that can change live Azure resources. These should require explicit scope and user approval before execution.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The cited commands are state-changing Azure operations. They are presented as examples, so they are risky only if executed without guardrails.

dangerous_patterns.title: Azure Resource Enumeration
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill uses subscription, resource group, Resource Graph, and Key Vault listing workflows. Use least-privilege permissions and avoid sharing full scan exports outside approved channels.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The enumeration workflow is explicit and central to the skill. It is expected behavior, but it can expose sensitive operational metadata.

content.user_title: Audit Azure Compliance and Key Vault Expiration
content.value_statement: Azure teams need fast visibility into compliance drift, risky resource settings, and expiring Key Vault assets. This skill guides structured Azure reviews with azqr, Resource Graph, and Key Vault metadata checks.
actual_capabilities: Guides Azure Quick Review scans for subscriptions and resource groups.
Explains how to prioritize azqr recommendations by security, reliability, cost, and operations impact.
Reviews Key Vault keys, secrets, and certificates for expiration status and missing expiration dates.
Provides Azure Resource Graph query patterns for tags, public access, locks, and role assignments.
Summarizes compliance findings into prioritized reports with remediation options.
limitations: Requires Azure authentication and sufficient read permissions before scans can run.
Does not deploy resources or apply remediations without separate user action.
May expose resource names and Key Vault metadata in scan outputs.
Reference commands may need adjustment for tenant, subscription, and policy requirements.

use_cases.title: Quarterly Cloud Compliance Review
use_cases.description: Security teams assess subscription posture, classify top findings, and prepare an action list for service owners.

use_cases.title: Key Vault Expiration Planning
use_cases.description: Cloud administrators identify expired or soon-expiring keys, secrets, and certificates before service failures occur.

use_cases.title: Governance Drift Investigation
use_cases.description: Platform teams use Resource Graph patterns to find missing tags, public endpoints, and unlocked resource groups.

prompt_templates.title: Check One Subscription
prompt_templates.prompt: Audit my Azure subscription for compliance issues. Summarize critical and high findings first, then list recommended next steps.

prompt_templates.title: Review Key Vault Expiration
prompt_templates.prompt: Check this Key Vault for expired items, items expiring within 30 days, and secrets without expiration dates. Do not print secret values.

prompt_templates.title: Investigate Governance Drift
prompt_templates.prompt: Use Azure Resource Graph patterns to find resources missing required tags and resources with public network access enabled.

prompt_templates.title: Build Executive Compliance Summary
prompt_templates.prompt: Review the azqr results, group findings by business impact, identify owners when possible, and create a remediation plan with validation steps.

output_examples.input: Run a compliance review for a production resource group.
output_examples.output: A prioritized assessment with scope, scan status, critical issues, high-risk configuration gaps, affected resource groups, and recommended owner actions.

output_examples.input: Check Key Vault items expiring in the next 30 days.
output_examples.output: A concise expiration report grouped by expired, expiring soon, no expiration, and healthy items. Secret values are not included.

output_examples.input: Find Azure governance drift across subscriptions.
output_examples.output: A governance summary covering missing tags, public access exposure, resource group lock gaps, and suggested Resource Graph follow-up queries.

best_practices: Run scans with the least Azure permissions needed for the assessment scope.
Confirm scope, tenant, and subscription before using commands that can read or change resources.
Redact secret values, subscription identifiers, and sensitive resource names before sharing reports.
anti_patterns: Do not run broad Key Vault secret show commands when metadata-only output is enough.
Do not apply remediation commands directly from examples without review and approval.
Do not treat azqr findings as final risk ratings without business context and owner validation.

faq.question: Does this skill change Azure resources?
faq.answer: The core audit workflow is review-focused. Some reference files include remediation commands that should require explicit approval before use.

faq.question: Can it audit Key Vault expiration?
faq.answer: Yes. It guides checks for expired items, upcoming expirations, disabled items, and resources without expiration dates.

faq.question: Does it need Azure permissions?
faq.answer: Yes. The user needs Azure authentication and enough read permissions for the selected subscription, resource group, or vault.

faq.question: Will it reveal secret values?
faq.answer: It should avoid printing secret values. Users should request metadata-only output when checking Key Vault secrets.

faq.question: Which AI tools can use it?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: Is this a replacement for Azure Policy?
faq.answer: No. It helps interpret scans and guide reviews, but Azure Policy remains the enforcement and compliance source.
